runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production
citations: Acemoglu (capital-skill complementarity / aggregate production function discussions)
Antras (CES production and factor substitution in macro/firm contexts)
Berndt and Christensen (1970s aggregate CES estimation tradition)
runs.reasoningSummary: My prior is that aggregate long-run capital-labor substitution is below unit elasticity but not extremely low, with the center around 0.8. I allow substantial uncertainty because estimates vary by sample, specification, and whether adjustment frictions are absorbed into the long-run elasticity.

I allow substantial uncertainty because estimates vary by sample, specification, and whether adjustment frictions are absorbed into the long-run elasticity."}

runs.interpretation: Long-run CES elasticity of substitution between capital and labor in U.S. aggregate private-sector production.
citations: Arrow, Chenery, Minhas, and Solow (1961) CES production function
Sato (1967) / Uzawa-style capital-labor substitution discussions
Acemoglu (2002) directed technical change and aggregate elasticity discussions
runs.reasoningSummary: My prior is that the long-run aggregate capital-labor elasticity in U.S. private production is likely below 1, but not extremely low. I center it around 0.8 with substantial uncertainty because estimates vary by horizon, specification, and whether adjustment costs, technology heterogeneity, and compositional change are absorbed into the aggregate CES interpretation.
